level-1-metrics-server.description: Running `kubectl top pods` returns an error and all metrics are unavailable across the cluster. There is a metrics-server Deployment in the namespace, but something about its configuration means it is not actually running any pods.
level-1-metrics-server.hint1: Check whether the metrics-server is actually running any pods: `kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=metrics-server`
level-1-metrics-server.hint2: If you see no pods (or 0/0 ready), inspect the Deployment directly: `kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep replicas`
level-1-metrics-server.hint3: The Deployment's `spec.replicas` field is set to 0. A Deployment with zero replicas deliberately runs no pods — the metrics-server is configured to exist but not to run.
level-1-metrics-server.reveal:
## What went wrong

The metrics-server Deployment has `replicas: 0`. This is a valid Kubernetes configuration — it means the Deployment exists but is scaled down to nothing. With no running pods, the Metrics API returns no data and `kubectl top` fails.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  replicas: 1
```

## Why this matters

The Metrics API (`metrics.k8s.io`) is not built into Kubernetes — it requires a separately deployed metrics-server. Without it, `kubectl top`, Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, and Vertical Pod Autoscaler all stop working. In production, this is typically installed via the metrics-server Helm chart or a cluster addon. The `replicas: 0` pattern is commonly used to disable a component without deleting it — reversible by scaling back up.

level-2-pod-events.description: A pod is failing to start but `kubectl logs` returns nothing at all — not even an error. The container has never successfully run, so there are no logs to read. Kubernetes recorded what happened, but you need to look in a different place than logs.
level-2-pod-events.hint1: Since logs are empty, read the pod's events instead: `kubectl describe pod events-pod -n k8smissions`. Scroll to the Events section at the bottom.
level-2-pod-events.hint2: The Events section shows Warning events with reasons like `ErrImagePull` or `ImagePullBackOff`. These include the exact error from the container runtime. Look at what image the pod is trying to pull.
level-2-pod-events.hint3: The pod's `image` field specifies a tag that does not exist in the registry. The container image reference needs to be updated to a valid tag.
level-2-pod-events.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod uses `nginx:broken-tag` — a tag that doesn't exist in Docker Hub. The container runtime tries to pull the image, gets a 404 from the registry, and the pod enters `ImagePullBackOff`. Because the container never started, there are no logs — the log buffer is empty.

## Fix

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: events-pod
    image: nginx:1.27.4
```

## Why this matters

When `kubectl logs` returns nothing, the container either hasn't started yet or exited before writing anything. In both cases, `kubectl describe pod` is your first tool — the Events section records every state transition with a reason and message. For image errors, the reason will be `ErrImagePull` (first attempt) or `ImagePullBackOff` (subsequent attempts with backoff). This diagnostic path — logs empty → check events — is the most common first step in pod debugging.

level-3-resource-top.description: The cluster node is experiencing CPU pressure and general performance is degraded. A pod is consuming far more CPU than it declared in its requests. You need to find it using metrics and then cap its consumption by adding a CPU limit.
level-3-resource-top.hint1: Use `kubectl top` to see which pod is consuming the most CPU right now: `kubectl top pods -n k8smissions`
level-3-resource-top.hint2: The cpu-hog pod will show extremely high CPU usage. Inspect its resource configuration: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=cpu-hog | grep -A10 Resource`
level-3-resource-top.hint3: The container has `resources.requests.cpu` set but no `resources.limits.cpu`. Without a CPU limit, the container can consume as much CPU as the node has available, starving everything else.
level-3-resource-top.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container runs `while true; do :; done` — a tight infinite loop that spins the CPU at 100%. It has a `requests.cpu: 100m` declaration (used for scheduling) but no `limits.cpu`, so the scheduler places it politely but the container then consumes all available CPU unchecked.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 32Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 200m
    memory: 64Mi
```

## Why this matters

In Kubernetes, `requests` is a scheduling hint — it tells the scheduler how much resource to reserve. `limits` is the enforcement ceiling — the kernel will throttle CPU or OOM-kill the container if it exceeds the limit. A pod without CPU limits is a noisy neighbor: it can absorb spare capacity without bound and degrade every other pod on the node. `kubectl top pods` is the real-time view; for historical usage, you need Prometheus or a similar metrics store.

level-4-log-aggregation.description: An application pod has restarted multiple times and you need to understand what caused the crashes. The current `kubectl logs` output is empty because the container just restarted and hasn't written anything yet. The evidence is in the previous container's logs.
level-4-log-aggregation.hint1: Check the pod's restart count: `kubectl get pod logs-pod -n k8smissions`. Then retrieve logs from the terminated container run: `kubectl logs logs-pod -n k8smissions --previous`
level-4-log-aggregation.hint2: The `--previous` flag returns logs from the last terminated container instance, not the currently running one. The output should show an error like 'exec: no such file or directory'. Now look at what the pod's `args` field tries to execute.
level-4-log-aggregation.hint3: The pod's `args` field references `/missing.sh` which does not exist inside the busybox image. Every time the container starts, the shell fails immediately with a 'not found' error and exits.
level-4-log-aggregation.reveal:
## What went wrong

The pod spec has `args: [/missing.sh]` — a script that doesn't exist in the busybox image. Every time the container starts, it tries to execute the missing file, gets a shell error, and exits with a non-zero code. Kubernetes restarts it, and the cycle repeats.

## Fix

Remove the broken args and replace with a valid command:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - name: logs-pod
    image: busybox:1.36
    command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - sleep 3600
```

## Why this matters

`kubectl logs --previous` is essential for debugging crash loops — it retrieves the stdout/stderr of the container that just died. Without it, a restarting pod's logs reset on every restart and you only see the current (empty) buffer. The flags `--since`, `--tail`, and `--timestamps` are also useful for narrowing down logs in a running container. In production, a centralized log aggregator (Loki, Elasticsearch, CloudWatch Logs) retains logs across restarts and is essential for post-incident review.

level-5-probe-types.description: Pods in this Deployment restart in an infinite loop and never stabilize. The liveness probe is configured, but it is checking an endpoint that only becomes available late in the startup sequence — so the probe kills the container during startup before the app ever becomes ready.
level-5-probe-types.hint1: Check the restart count and read the probe failure events: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=probe-app | grep -A15 Liveness`
level-5-probe-types.hint2: The liveness probe is checking `/api/v1/ready` — an endpoint designed to report readiness, not health. This endpoint responds slowly during startup. When the probe fires early and gets a non-200 response, Kubernetes treats the container as unhealthy and kills it.
level-5-probe-types.hint3: The `livenessProbe.httpGet.path` is set to `/api/v1/ready`, which is a readiness endpoint. Liveness probes should point to a lightweight, always-fast `/healthz` endpoint. The `/api/v1/ready` check belongs in a separate `readinessProbe` block.
level-5-probe-types.reveal:
## What went wrong

Liveness and readiness probes have different jobs. The liveness probe asks: 'is the container alive and should it continue running?' The readiness probe asks: 'is the container ready to receive traffic?' Using a readiness endpoint for liveness means that any moment the app isn't fully ready (including startup) causes Kubernetes to kill and restart the container.

## Fix

```yaml
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /api/v1/ready
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 5
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 5
```

## Why this matters

The three probe types serve three distinct purposes:
- **livenessProbe**: Is the container stuck or deadlocked? Failure → restart the container.
- **readinessProbe**: Is the container ready to serve traffic? Failure → remove from Service endpoints (no restart).
- **startupProbe**: Has the container finished its slow initialization? Failure → don't start liveness/readiness yet.

A liveness endpoint should always be fast and cheap — a simple in-memory check. Never point liveness at an endpoint that depends on a database, cache, or slow initialization.

level-6-restart-cause.description: A pod shows 8 restarts in the past hour but the current logs show nothing — no crash message, no application error. The restarts happen silently. You need to read the pod's history to determine what actually killed it each time.
level-6-restart-cause.hint1: Inspect the pod's last state to see how the previous container exited: `kubectl describe pod oom-pod -n k8smissions`. Look at the `Last State` section near the top.
level-6-restart-cause.hint2: The `Last State` section should show `Exit Code: 137`. Exit code 137 means the process was killed with SIGKILL — the kernel's signal for an OOM (Out Of Memory) kill. Now check the memory limit: `kubectl get pod oom-pod -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 limits`
level-6-restart-cause.hint3: The container's `resources.limits.memory` is set to 64Mi, but the Python command inside allocates 128 MB of data. Every time the container starts, it exceeds the memory limit and is killed by the kernel.
level-6-restart-cause.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Python command allocates 128 × 1MB strings (128MB total), but the container's memory limit is only 64Mi. When memory usage crosses the limit, the Linux kernel OOM-kills the process with SIGKILL (signal 9), which produces exit code 137 (128 + 9). No application-level error is written — the process is killed externally, not by a crash.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: 256Mi
  requests:
    memory: 64Mi
```

## Why this matters

Exit codes tell you the cause of a container termination:
- `0` — clean exit
- `1` — application crash (unhandled exception, etc.)
- `137` — OOM kill (SIGKILL from kernel)
- `143` — graceful shutdown (SIGTERM)

OOM kills leave no log output because the process is interrupted externally. Always check `kubectl describe pod` → Last State → Exit Code before looking for logs when a pod keeps restarting silently.

level-7-node-pressure.description: Pods from multiple workloads are being unexpectedly evicted from the node. One Deployment is running several replicas of a memory-intensive command with no resource cap, pushing the node into MemoryPressure. Until the pressure is relieved, the kubelet will keep evicting pods.
level-7-node-pressure.hint1: Check the node conditions to confirm the pressure: `kubectl describe node kind-control-plane | grep -A10 Conditions`
level-7-node-pressure.hint2: You should see `MemoryPressure: True`. Now find which pod is responsible: `kubectl top pods -n k8smissions`. Then inspect the memory-hog Deployment to see how many replicas are running and what each container does.
level-7-node-pressure.hint3: The `memory-hog` Deployment has 4 replicas with no `resources.limits.memory`. Each container runs a `dd` command writing large amounts of data, and with 4 replicas and no cap, they collectively exhaust the node's available memory.
level-7-node-pressure.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Deployment runs 4 pods, each executing `dd if=/dev/zero` — a command that continuously writes null bytes and consumes memory. With no `limits.memory`, the combined usage pushes the node into MemoryPressure. When the kubelet detects this condition, it starts evicting pods in QoS order (BestEffort first, then Burstable, then Guaranteed) until pressure is relieved.

## Fix

Reduce replicas and add memory limits:

```yaml
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: memory-hog
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - sleep 3600
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: 64Mi
          limits:
            memory: 128Mi
```

## Why this matters

Node conditions — `MemoryPressure`, `DiskPressure`, `PIDPressure` — trigger the kubelet's eviction manager. Pods without limits have `BestEffort` QoS and are evicted first. You can prevent this by always setting both requests and limits. If a node is under sustained pressure, consider cordoning it (`kubectl cordon`) to stop new scheduling while you diagnose.

level-8-probe-tuning.description: A slow-starting application pod keeps restarting before it finishes initializing. The liveness probe is correctly written for the steady-state running condition, but nothing is protecting the startup window. The probe fires too soon and kills the container before the app is ready.
level-8-probe-tuning.hint1: Check the probe configuration and look for restart events: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=slow-app | grep -A15 Liveness`
level-8-probe-tuning.hint2: The events show the liveness probe failing during startup. Look at `initialDelaySeconds` — that is when the first probe fires. If the app takes longer than that to start, every startup attempt will be killed by the liveness probe.
level-8-probe-tuning.hint3: The pod spec has a `livenessProbe` but no `startupProbe`. Without a startup probe, the liveness check begins after `initialDelaySeconds` regardless of whether the application has finished starting up.
level-8-probe-tuning.reveal:
## What went wrong

The app takes time to initialize before its `/healthz` endpoint is available. The liveness probe fires after only 5 seconds (`initialDelaySeconds: 5`), gets no response, and kills the container. This repeats forever.

## Fix

Add a `startupProbe` that gives the app a protected startup window:

```yaml
startupProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  failureThreshold: 30
  periodSeconds: 5
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 5
```

With `failureThreshold: 30` and `periodSeconds: 5`, the startup probe gives the app up to 150 seconds to become available before giving up. While the startup probe is active, the liveness probe is disabled.

## Why this matters

`startupProbe` was added specifically to solve the slow-start problem. Before it existed, engineers had to set very large `initialDelaySeconds` on the liveness probe — which delayed failure detection for the entire pod lifetime. The startup probe runs first and hands off to the liveness probe once it succeeds. The math: `failureThreshold × periodSeconds` = maximum startup budget. For Java apps, this is often 60–180 seconds.

level-9-exec-debugging.description: A pod is Running and passing health checks, but the application returns 500 errors with no useful log output. The problem is in the configuration file the app loaded at startup — not in the code itself. You need to exec into the live container and read what configuration it actually has.
level-9-exec-debugging.hint1: Exec into the running container to inspect its config: `kubectl exec -it debug-pod -n k8smissions -- /bin/sh`. Then read the mounted config file: `cat /app/config.json`
level-9-exec-debugging.hint2: The config file should show `DB_HOST` set to a raw IP address like `10.0.0.99`. In Kubernetes, pod IP addresses are ephemeral — they change every time a pod restarts. Services should always be referenced by their DNS name.
level-9-exec-debugging.hint3: The ConfigMap's `config.json` value has `DB_HOST` set to a hardcoded IP address. It should be set to the cluster DNS name of the database Service: `database.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local`.
level-9-exec-debugging.reveal:
## What went wrong

The ConfigMap contains `"DB_HOST": "10.0.0.99"` — a hardcoded pod IP. Pod IPs are ephemeral: they are assigned at creation and released on deletion. The IP that was valid when the ConfigMap was written may belong to a completely different pod or be unroutable now.

## Fix

Update the ConfigMap to use the Service DNS name:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: k8smissions
data:
  config.json: '{"DB_HOST":"database.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local","DB_PORT":5432}'
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes Services provide stable DNS names that survive pod restarts. The full DNS format is `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. Within the same namespace, `<service-name>` alone is sufficient. `kubectl exec` is the primary tool for live debugging — running commands inside a container without restarting it. For containers without shells (distroless images), use `kubectl debug` to attach an ephemeral sidecar container with debugging tools.

level-10-cluster-events.description: A production incident occurred recently. The `incident-app` Deployment had pods repeatedly crashing. Your task is to reconstruct what happened using cluster events, identify the root cause from the event timeline, and then fix the underlying configuration.
level-10-cluster-events.hint1: Build the incident timeline by reading cluster events sorted by time: `kubectl get events -n k8smissions --sort-by=.lastTimestamp`
level-10-cluster-events.hint2: Look for OOMKilled events in the output — these indicate the container was killed by the kernel for exceeding its memory limit. Then check the current memory limit on the Deployment: `kubectl describe deployment incident-app -n k8smissions | grep -A5 Limits`
level-10-cluster-events.hint3: The container's `resources.limits.memory` is set too low. The application's memory usage exceeds the limit, triggering repeated OOM kills that appear as restarts in the event stream.
level-10-cluster-events.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container has `limits.memory: 64Mi` but consumes more than that at runtime. Each time memory usage crosses the limit, the kernel sends SIGKILL (exit code 137). The event stream shows the restart history: `Killing container with id ... OOMKilled`.

## Fix

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: 32Mi
  limits:
    memory: 256Mi
```

## Why this matters

Cluster events are a first-class observability tool and the primary source of truth for incident timelines. `kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp` gives you a chronological view. Key event fields: `reason` (what happened), `message` (detail), `count` (how many times), `firstTime`/`lastTime`. Events have a default TTL of 1 hour — for longer retention you need an event exporter (e.g., `kube-event-exporter` or writing to a log store). The SRE discipline of writing post-mortems starts with this event timeline: what happened, when, and why.

level-11-readiness-path.description: A Deployment has been running for ten minutes but all pods show `0/1 READY`. No traffic is reaching them through the Service even though the containers are up. The readiness probe is failing because it is checking a path that this web server does not serve.
level-11-readiness-path.hint1: Check the readiness probe configuration: `kubectl describe pod -n k8smissions -l app=web-app | grep -A10 Readiness`
level-11-readiness-path.hint2: The probe is checking `/healthz` on port 80. Nginx does not serve `/healthz` by default — that path returns a 404, which the readiness probe treats as a failure. Check what nginx actually serves by looking at the image documentation or testing the path directly.
level-11-readiness-path.hint3: The `readinessProbe.httpGet.path` is set to `/healthz`, but this nginx image doesn't serve that path. Nginx returns a 404, the probe fails, and the pods never become Ready. The correct path for the default nginx welcome page is `/`.
level-11-readiness-path.reveal:
## What went wrong

The readiness probe sends `GET /healthz HTTP/1.1` to port 80. Nginx has no `/healthz` route, so it returns 404. A non-2xx response counts as a probe failure. After `failureThreshold` consecutive failures, the pod is marked NotReady and removed from the Service's endpoint list.

## Fix

```yaml
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /
    port: 80
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 5
```

## Why this matters

Readiness probes control Service routing — a pod that fails readiness gets no traffic, but is not restarted. This is the right behavior during initialization or when a dependency is temporarily unavailable. The path you probe must actually exist on the server. For production nginx deployments, add a dedicated `/healthz` location block in your nginx.conf, or use a TCP probe (`tcpSocket`) as a simpler alternative: if nginx is listening, the port is open.

level-12-liveness-threshold.description: A pod's RESTARTS counter keeps climbing every few minutes even though the application is working correctly and the logs show no errors. The restarts are being triggered by the health check configuration — a single transient delay is enough to cause a restart.
level-12-liveness-threshold.hint1: Look at the liveness probe configuration: `kubectl describe pod fragile-app -n k8smissions | grep -A10 Liveness`
level-12-liveness-threshold.hint2: Note the `failureThreshold` value. With `failureThreshold: 1`, a single missed probe check — caused by any momentary system load or scheduling delay — immediately triggers a container restart. The Kubernetes default is 3.
level-12-liveness-threshold.hint3: The `livenessProbe.failureThreshold` is set to 1. This is far too sensitive. One transient probe timeout is enough to kill and restart the container, causing the constant restart loop you observe.
level-12-liveness-threshold.reveal:
## What went wrong

With `failureThreshold: 1`, the liveness probe has zero tolerance for transient delays. Any momentary hiccup — a busy node, a GC pause, a network blip — causes a probe miss, which immediately triggers a pod restart. This creates a cycle of unnecessary restarts that can cascade into service disruption.

## Fix

```yaml
livenessProbe:
  exec:
    command:
    - ls
    - /
  initialDelaySeconds: 10
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3
```

## Why this matters

The four probe timing fields work together:
- `initialDelaySeconds`: how long to wait before the first probe
- `periodSeconds`: how often to probe
- `failureThreshold`: consecutive failures before action is taken
- `successThreshold`: consecutive successes needed to mark healthy again (default: 1)

Total time before restart = `failureThreshold × periodSeconds`. With the fix above, the pod tolerates 30 seconds of probe failures before being restarted — enough to survive any transient delay in a healthy cluster. The Kubernetes defaults (3 failures, 10s period) are sensible starting points for most applications.

level-13-startup-probe.description: An application pod keeps restarting during container startup. The app takes 120 seconds to complete initialization before it signals readiness, but the liveness probe is configured with timing that kills the container after only 40 seconds — long before initialization can finish.
level-13-startup-probe.hint1: Check the probe configuration and do the timing math: `kubectl describe pod slow-java-app -n k8smissions | grep -A15 Liveness`
level-13-startup-probe.hint2: Calculate the total time before the liveness probe kills the pod: `initialDelaySeconds + (failureThreshold × periodSeconds)`. Compare that number to how long the app actually needs to boot (look at the command — it sleeps for 120 seconds before creating `/tmp/ready`).
level-13-startup-probe.hint3: The pod has no `startupProbe`. Without one, the liveness probe begins after `initialDelaySeconds: 10` and kills the container after 3 × 10s = 30 more seconds — at 40s total. The app needs 120s to boot, so it is always killed 80 seconds too early.
level-13-startup-probe.reveal:
## What went wrong

The app sleeps 120 seconds before touching `/tmp/ready`. The liveness probe starts at 10s and kills the container at 40s (10 + 3×10). The container is killed 80 seconds before it can ever succeed. Without a startup probe, there is no way to give a slow-starting app a protected initialization window.

## Fix

Add a `startupProbe` that covers the full startup window:

```yaml
startupProbe:
  exec:
    command:
    - test
    - -f
    - /tmp/ready
  failureThreshold: 30
  periodSeconds: 5
livenessProbe:
  exec:
    command:
    - test
    - -f
    - /tmp/ready
  initialDelaySeconds: 0
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3
```

`30 × 5s = 150s` startup budget — enough for the 120s boot. While the startup probe is active, the liveness probe is suspended entirely.

## Why this matters

The startup probe solves a fundamental tension: slow-starting apps need time, but liveness probes need to act fast when a running app deadlocks. The startup probe separates these two concerns cleanly. Once it succeeds once, it stops running and hands off to liveness. This pattern is essential for JVM-based services, databases loading large datasets, and any application with a non-trivial initialization sequence.

level-14-log-level.description: Container logs are flooding with thousands of DEBUG-level lines per second. The logging pipeline cannot keep up, meaningful log entries are buried under noise, and storage costs are spiking. A single environment variable controls the verbosity and it is set to the wrong value for production.
level-14-log-level.hint1: Check the current log output: `kubectl logs logger-app -n k8smissions | head -20`. Then look at the pod's environment variables: `kubectl get pod logger-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 env`
level-14-log-level.hint2: You should see `LOG_LEVEL: DEBUG` in the environment variables. DEBUG logging emits every internal state change and is appropriate only during active development. Look at the manifest's `env` block to find where to change this.
level-14-log-level.hint3: The `env` block sets the `LOG_LEVEL` environment variable to `DEBUG`. Changing this value to `INFO` will reduce log volume to only meaningful operational events while keeping errors and warnings visible.
level-14-log-level.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container runs with `LOG_LEVEL: DEBUG`, emitting approximately one log line every 100ms (~600 lines/minute, ~864,000 lines/day). At this volume, important log entries (errors, warnings) are hidden in noise and log storage costs become significant.

## Fix

```yaml
env:
- name: LOG_LEVEL
  value: INFO
```

## Why this matters

Log levels define the minimum severity of events to emit. The conventional hierarchy: `DEBUG < INFO < WARN < ERROR < FATAL`. In production, `INFO` is the standard — it captures application lifecycle events and errors without debug noise. Use a `ConfigMap` to manage the log level value so you can change it across deployments without rebuilding the image. Some teams implement dynamic log level changes via an API endpoint, allowing temporary DEBUG logging during incident investigation without a pod restart.

level-15-prometheus-annotations.description: A service is emitting Prometheus metrics on port 9090 but they never appear in monitoring dashboards. The pod is Running, the port is exposed, and the metrics endpoint is working — but Prometheus has no way to discover this pod because the annotation-based discovery configuration is entirely missing.
level-15-prometheus-annotations.hint1: Check what annotations the pod currently has: `kubectl get pod metrics-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 annotations`
level-15-prometheus-annotations.hint2: There are no annotations at all. Prometheus uses specific pod annotations to discover which pods to scrape. Without `prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'`, Prometheus ignores this pod entirely regardless of whether the metrics endpoint works.
level-15-prometheus-annotations.hint3: The pod's `metadata.annotations` block is missing entirely. Three annotations are needed: `prometheus.io/scrape` to enable discovery, `prometheus.io/port` to specify which port to scrape, and `prometheus.io/path` to specify the metrics endpoint path.
level-15-prometheus-annotations.reveal:
## What went wrong

Prometheus uses a pod discovery mechanism that reads annotations to find scrape targets. Without the `prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'` annotation, the pod is invisible to Prometheus — it is never added to the scrape queue regardless of what port it exposes.

## Fix

Add the three Prometheus scrape annotations to the pod metadata:

```yaml
metadata:
  name: metrics-app
  namespace: k8smissions
  labels:
    app: metrics-app
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'
    prometheus.io/port: '9090'
    prometheus.io/path: /metrics
```

## Why this matters

Prometheus' annotation-based pod discovery is configured in the Prometheus `scrape_config` with a `kubernetes_sd_configs` block. When enabled, it watches all pods in the cluster and filters to those annotated with `prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'`. The `port` and `path` annotations tell Prometheus where to make the HTTP request. This pattern is the foundation of metrics-based observability: once annotations are in place, any Prometheus-compatible dashboard (Grafana) can display the metrics without any additional configuration. For more structured metric discovery, `ServiceMonitor` resources (from the Prometheus Operator) provide a Kubernetes-native alternative to annotations.
